details.text: Derivative: أَلَنَّا from لِين and it means to soften. Here, it means that Allah made the iron easy or soft for him to mould and shape, like it was dough or putty. It does not mean the ability to melt the iron but rather an ability that was special for Dawud (as) where he could mould metal as if it was putty.

Derivative: أَوِّبِي from أَوَّبَ - تَعْوِيب which means to cause something to come back and return. أَوَّاب is the person who keeps coming back over and over again i.e., to Allah. It also means to reverberate, echo or sing and chant in a vibrant or quavering tone. تَعْوِيب is used for a part of a poem or song, like the chorus, in which others join in with the poet or singer. Here it refers to the mountains and birds joining in with Dawud (as) in his chanting and singing when he glorifies Allah and declares His perfection (تَسْبيح).
Derivative: سَابِغَاتٍ refers to wide and long armour that protects the vital areas i.e., a complete coat of mail that covers most of the body.

- “And measure precisely in respect to the manufacture of the mail/armour (with iron links - chainmail)” i.e., manufacture it well, with precision, excellence and careful planning or thought behind it.
Derivative: أَسَلْنَا means to make flow but in this context (i.e., that of copper metal) it implies that it was melted to form a spring of copper (عَيْنَ الْقِطْرِ).
Derivative: الْقِطْرِ means نُحَاس which is copper.

- “The distance it travels in the morning is a month’s distance and the distance it travels to return in the late afternoon or last part of the day before the sun sets, is a month’s distance i.e., in accordance with your normal travel distance in a month”.
- فِي غَدُوِهِ وَرَواحِهِ is also an expression to refer to goings and comings and hence in can refer to the outward travelling distance (or journey) and the return travel distance (or journey).

Derivative: مَحَارِيبَ is the plural of مِحْرَاب (Mihrab). A Mihrab is a niche in the wall of a Masjid that indicates the direction of the Qiblah. It is also used to refer to a building that is grand, high and spacious like a castle or palace. It is said that it refers to a high building that has stairs. It also refers to buildings that are made for the purpose of worship.
Derivative: الْجَوَابِ is the plural of جَابِيَة which refers to a large basin or pond.
Derivative: رَّاسِيَاتٍ means firmly fixed in place i.e., due to the size and weight.
Derivative: جِفَانٍ is the plural of جَفْنَة and refers to something bowl shaped like a cauldron.
Derivative: قُدُورٍ is the plural of قِدْر and refers to a huge pot (for food).
Derivative: تَمَاثِيلَ is the plural of تِمْثَال and refers to monuments or statues that are carved from stone.

Derivative: مِنسَأَة is an عَصا meaning staff or stick from نَسَأَ which means to delay or defer and is used for stick or staff (مِنسَأَة) as that is used to ward something away to stop it encroaching.

Derivative: الْعَرِمِ can refer to a dam which broke leading to the destructive flood waters. It has also been said to be the name of the valley from which the flood water came just as it has been said to mean a very powerful flood of water otherwise known as a storm flood.
Derivative: أَثْلٍ refers to Tamarisk which is devoid of beneficial fruit and symbolic of a degraded environment.
Derivative: خَمْطٍ sour, bitter and very unpleasant.
Derivative: نُجَازِي means to repay, give retribution and punish.
Derivative: ظَاهِرَةً means that appeared. Here it refers to towns that were in between the major towns, where one ended another would appear and become visible.
- “And we made of them stories” i.e., traces of them disappeared where they were only spoken about by those who came after as stories from the past.
“And we utterly tore them up or ripped them apart” which means that we divided and dispersed them completely whereby they lost their power, influence, standing and previous benefits.

- غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ and غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ have been mentioned dozens of times in the Qur’an in the order of forgiveness first followed by mercy, however, this is the only time that the mercy precedes the forgiveness الرَّحِيمُ الْغَفُورُ.
- This may be indicative of the Surah and how Allah continued to show His mercy to certain nations and then eventually when they exhausted that, Allah asked them to seek forgiveness as a last chance for their nation.
- In addition, the verse discusses universal and general matters that are inclusive and encompassing of humans and other creation. The mercy (رَحْمَة) of Allah is universal and encompasses everything, whilst His forgiveness (مَغْفِرَة) is specific to humankind and Jinn as they can sin and are in need of forgiveness. As such, it has been argued that this may be a reason for placing الرَّحِيمُ first before الْغَفُورُ. In the verses where forgiveness is mentioned first, the subject matter is the human being.
Derivative: زَعَمْتُم from زَعَمَ to claim or assume, normally implies a false assumption or claim.

Derivative: فُزِّعَ can mean to strike fear and terror. It can also means to remove fear and terror. It is similar to مَرَّضَ which can mean to make ill or sick just as it can mean to nurse and tend to someone who is sick. This is specific to the فَعَّلَ form. Here the meaning is for the terror and fear to be removed (from the hearts).
Derivative: يَفْتَحُ means to open and also to pass the final decision or judgement, particularly when used with بَيْنَ (between).

Derivative: كَافَّةً – This means “all” or “the entirety” and is the حال (describing the state) of the people (النَّاس) and has been placed earlier in the order of the sentence to add extra significance and emphasis. It emphasises that the Messenger ﷺ was sent for all of mankind in its entirety i.e., there is nobody anywhere except that Muhammad ﷺ was sent to them as a Basheer (bearer of good news) and Nadheer (warner).

Derivative: يَعْزُبُ from عَزَبَ and it means to be far away and distant where sight cannot reach. It also means to be hidden away from or to escape and slip someone’s mind. لَا يَعْزُبُ means that nothing is hidden away or escapes.

- The use of يَعْزُبُ (to be distant and hidden) in the context of مِثْقَالُ ذَرَّةٍ (the weight of an atom or speck) implies that something that you can’t even see when it is close to you is not hidden from Allah when it is distant i.e., nothing, regardless of how small it is, is hidden from Allah in the heavens and the earth.
Derivative: مِّيعَادُ means appointment from وَعَدَ to promise and implies a serious and important appointment or meeting date.
- Literally “Some of them returning words or speech to others (from them)” which means they were arguing amongst themselves and throwing accusations against one another.
- “If you could (only) see …” This statement is used multiple times in the Qur’an and is never provided an answer (جواب). It is always left …

Derivative: مُتْرَفُوهَا from أَتْرَفَ. تَرِفَ means to have a life full of luxuries and blessings. أَتْرَفَ means that he was deceived or deluded by the blessings and become over obsessed and indulged. مُتْرَف is someone who has so much luxury in life that he has lost sight of reality and lives in his bubble of luxury i.e., those who have become corrupted by wealth and luxury.

the verse begins addressing the plural when discussing those who make claims to success by the amount of their wealth or number of children and then reverts to the singular when addressing the one who believes and acts righteously. it also attributes double reward for the one who believes and acts righteously. when combined this could indicate to the one who believes and acts righteously within an environment that is not supportive of belief and righteous acts and where the majority are disbelievers.
Derivative: يُخْلِفُ from أَخْلَفَ which means to be turned back around to you and to leave behind. Here its meaning is to recompense and compensate.

- “Those will have forgiveness and noble provision” – Here, the مَّغْفِرَةٌ (forgiveness) precedes the رِزْقٌ كَرِيمٌ (Noble provision i.e., Jannah) as the first is the prerequisite of receiving the latter.
Derivative: يَمْلِكُ from مَلَكَ which means to own, possess and also to control and have the power or capability to do something. Here, it refers to the power, control and capability.
Derivative: تُكَذِّبُونَ from كَذَّبَ which means to be in denial of or to call a lie.
Derivative: إِفْكٌ is a vicious lie or slanderous fabrication.
Derivative: مُفْتَرًى from اِفْتَرَى which means to make something up or to fabricate.
Derivative: نَكِير refers to the terrible (unspeakable) consequence, reproach, condemnation and punishment.
Derivative: يَقْذِفُ from قَذَفَ which means to hurl, cast or throw. It is used in relation to arrows, stones and speech.
Derivative: يُبْدِئُ means to initiate, bring or begin something new.
“And falsehood can neither bring or repeat it” is an expression meaning that it has none of the qualities to remain in existence and that it is doomed to perish.

Derivative: رِجْز in origin refers to something that convulses and is unstable. In the Qur’an it refers to the punishment that convulses because of its severity. It is also used to refer to the evil whispers of Shaytan, to a war chant that strikes fear into the enemy and to the worshipping of idols (أَوْثان).

Derivative: مُعَاجِزِينَ – عَجَزَ means to be incapable and is the opposite of قَدَرَ (to be capable). عَاجِز is the one who is incapable and قَادِر is the one who is capable. مُعَاجِز which is being used here means somebody who is doing his best to make somebody else incapable, fail or not produce results – close in meaning to trying to undermine and sabotage something or somebody’s efforts. If they succeed in making it fail or rendering something incapable, then they are called مُعْجِز but if they are making efforts and trying to make something fail or to render something incapable, then they are مُعَاجِز.
Derivative: فَوْتَ from فَاتَ. فَوْت refers to an escape or a place flee to.
- “And if you could only see …”
Derivative: التَّنَاوُشُ refers to the reach that is as far as an arm can reach. Here it refers to reaching and attaining Iman (belief).
- “They used to throw or cast at the unseen” i.e., they would attack with their mouths that which they had no knowledge of with their insults, lies, defamation and slander.
Derivative: أَشْيَاعِ refers to groups which resemble them from the previous nations i.e., in their disbelief, conduct and reaction to the Messengers.
- This means those who are in doubt (شَكٍّ) and who cause and spread doubt and suspicion in others (مُّرِيبٍ).
Derivative: مُزِّقْتُمْ from مَزَّقَ – يُمَزِّقُ – تَمْزِيق means to rip apart, shred or tear to pieces.
Derivative: جِنَّة means to be touched by a Jinn which is an expression for being afflicted by madness or insanity.
Derivative: نَخْسِفْ from خَسَفَ when linked to the ground, it means to make them disappear and go into the ground i.e., to be made to sink or be swallowed into the ground leaving no trace.

Derivative: مُّنِيبٍ means to turn back. أَنَابَ – يُنِيبُ – إِنابَة means to turn back and return in a spiritual sense i.e., to turn back and return to Allah and His path through repentance and righteous deeds.
Derivative: نُسْقِطْ from أَسْقَطَ which means to drop something (heavy) from a height.
